briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon/evening. We will now begin the climate briefing for April 25, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Global Climate Crisis and Societal Responses
sections.content: International news reported today clearly demonstrates that the global climate crisis is already severely impacting human lives and ecosystems. Wildfires are burning longer, even during nighttime hours [11], and climate change is already altering how people work, eat, and maintain their health [12]. Arctic seals are facing a double burden from climate change and persistent pollutants [13], and in Antarctica, an unprecedented phenomenon occurred with temperatures rising by 28°C due to a winter heatwave [17]. Amidst these rising temperatures and climate change concerns, shifts in global travel patterns are also being observed, such as a 'coolcation' boom where tourists are heading to colder destinations [16]. Meanwhile, research from the UK suggests that criminalizing non-violent climate protesters may be counterproductive, indicating the complexity of societal discussions surrounding climate action [21].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Policy and Agricultural Sector Shifts in Climate Response
sections.content: Domestically, policy shifts for responding to the climate crisis are actively underway. In Yeosu City, 'UNFCCC Climate Week' has commenced, initiating global climate response discussions [1]. Chungnam Province is also strengthening its regional efforts by reviewing the progress of its carbon neutrality master plan [5]. Notably, a major paradigm shift in agricultural policy is gaining momentum, driven by the recognition that agriculture cannot be sustained without addressing the climate crisis [3]. Ahead of the 2026 local elections, Jeju also saw the proposal of pledges for future preservation through carbon-neutral agriculture [6]. At the heart of all these efforts, the Presidential Committee on the National Climate Crisis Response is playing a crucial role [4, 7].

sections.title: 🌱 Scientific and Technological Innovation and Renewable Energy Expansion
sections.content: Progress in scientific and technological innovation and the renewable energy sector for overcoming the climate crisis is also noteworthy. Professor Mosab Kaseem of Sejong University's Department of Nano-New Materials Engineering is conducting research that transcends scientific boundaries in two critical areas: cancer treatment and carbon neutrality [8]. Internationally, Aston University has made significant strides towards achieving net-zero goals with the groundbreaking of a new energy center [20], and Telangana State is set to announce renewable energy cooperation with Norway in March 2026 [9]. Domestic company Hanwha Q CELLS also plans to lead the energy transition by unveiling its next-generation solar technology and business portfolio at the 2026 International Green Energy Expo [10], demonstrating that technological advancements for a sustainable future are accelerating.

sections.title: 🌱 Climate Education and Biodiversity Conservation Efforts
sections.content: Efforts for climate crisis education and biodiversity conservation for future generations are also consistently ongoing. Domestically, Geumwang Kindergarten is operating a 'Visiting Climate School' as part of its climate crisis response education, instilling the importance of climate action from an early age [2]. Internationally, through the 'Birds are Global Citizens' project, Indigenous communities in Australia and Alaska are collaborating on a new knowledge-sharing project to track the epic journeys of migratory birds and ensure their survival [22]. This demonstrates ongoing practical efforts to recognize the vulnerability of ecosystems and pass on a healthy planet to the next generation.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the severity of the climate crisis, along with various domestic and international efforts to address it. Only sustained interest and participation can safeguard the future for all of us. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: Criminalisation of climate protesters in UK is counterproductive, research finds
articles.description: Study of 1,300 campaigners finds arrests, fines and jail terms increase determination of activists to take direct actionThe criminalisation of direct action climate protests in the UK is counterproductive and increases the determination of activists to undertake disruptive demonstrations, according to a study of 1,300 campaigners.New findings suggest arrests, fines and lengthy prison sentences given to nonviolent climate protesters who have blocked roads or damaged buildings may actually radical

summary.phenomenon: The criminalization of direct action protests, including arrests, fines, and prison sentences for nonviolent climate protesters who have blocked roads or damaged buildings in the UK, has been observed and confirmed by a study of 1,300 activists.
summary.outlook: The study suggests that such criminalization may increase activists' determination to engage in disruptive protests and could actually radicalize them further.

articles.original_title: ‘The birds are a global citizen’: Indigenous groups in Australia and Alaska team up to track a feathered adventurer’s epic journey
articles.description: A new knowledge-sharing project aims to ensure the survival of the migratory short-tailed shearwaterShort-tailed shearwaters used to blacken the skies on the south-west coast of Australia, so abundant were they in their coastal homes each Djilba season – the time in the calendar of the Noongar peoples between August and September, when days shift from blustery cold and wet winds to warmer weather.In Wudjari Noongar, the language of the traditional owners of this place they call Kepa Kurl, but wh

summary.phenomenon: While short-tailed shearwaters were historically abundant enough to blacken the skies during the Djilba season (August-September) on Australia's southwest coast, a new knowledge-sharing project is now underway between Indigenous peoples in Australia and Alaska to ensure the survival of this migratory bird.
summary.outlook: This knowledge-sharing project aims to ensure the survival of the migratory short-tailed shearwater.
